result:
On my balcony, a shallow wooden box has become a tiny city farm. I started it out of boredom during a long rainy spring, dropping a few tomato seeds into damp soil and hoping they would do something. They did more than that: the seedlings climbed, twisted, and demanded attention every day. I learned to read leaves like weather reports. Pale edges meant more fertilizer, drooping meant thirst, and bright new shoots meant the plant was delighted.

The garden is small, but it changes how I see the city. The supermarket aisle feels less mysterious when I have watched a pepper thicken from a green arrow to a glossy bell. I notice sunlight patterns on the brick wall, and I leave the window open to invite in pollinators. When a bumblebee visits, I feel absurdly proud, as if I had bribed it with good architecture.

There are failures too: basil that bolted, strawberries that tasted like water, one pot that became a mosquito swamp. Yet the small routines anchor my mornings. I check soil moisture before checking messages. I mist the herbs while the kettle heats. The plants do not care about my deadlines, and that indifference is oddly comforting.

A tiny farm won’t fix the world, but it makes my corner of it softer and more alive. That seems like a reasonable harvest. Sometimes friends bring me cuttings, and we trade advice like recipes, building a quiet community above the street noise. It feels like belonging to seasons.

I check soil moisture before checking messages. I mist the herbs while the kettle heats. The plants do not care about my deadlines, and that indifference is oddly comforting.
